question: Weyl (two-component) spinors are the irreducible spinor reps of the Lorentz group SL(2, $\mathbb C$). What's the difference between left ($\psi_L$) and right ($\psi_R$) Weyl spinors?

options: $\psi_L$ in $(\tfrac12, 0)$, $\psi_R$ in $(0, \tfrac12)$ of Lorentz; $\gamma^5 = \mp 1$ eigenstates; Dirac $\psi = (\psi_L, \psi_R)^T$
Left and right Weyl spinors differ only in spatial orientation; they share the same underlying Lorentz representation
Left and right Weyl spinors are physically equivalent and can be converted into each other by a simple complex phase
Only one Weyl representation exists in 4D spacetime; the labels L and R are merely conventional notational tags
passive.explanation: Weyl reps are complex 2-dim and inequivalent (related by parity/complex conjugation). Dirac spinor = direct sum of L+R (4-dim). Mass term $m\bar\psi\psi = m(\bar\psi_L\psi_R + \bar\psi_R\psi_L)$ couples L and R, so massive fermions cannot stay purely chiral. Massless fermions (neutrinos, in early SM) can be purely L (the SM electroweak interactions only see L for leptons and quarks). Notation: $\psi_L = \chi^\alpha$, $\psi_R = \bar\chi_{\dot\alpha}$ in van der Waerden's dotted-index convention.

active.prompt: Match each spinor representation to its property:

pairs.definition: Massless fermions; chirality eigenstate; minimal Lorentz spinor rep; SM left-handed leptons
Charged massive fermions (electron, muon, quark); both chiralities present
Self-conjugate; neutral, possibly with mass; candidate for neutrino if lepton number violated

pairs.term: Symplectic Majorana (in higher dim or extended SUSY)
pairs.definition: Reality condition in dimensions where standard Majorana is impossible; uses 'symplectic' index

active.explanation: Number of independent spinor components varies with spacetime dimension (Bott periodicity). 4D: Dirac 4, Weyl 2, Majorana 4 (real) = 2 complex. 10D string theory: Majorana-Weyl 8 real (= 16 real Dirac/2). 11D supergravity: Majorana 32. These count fermionic degrees of freedom needed for supersymmetry.

question: Van der Waerden's dotted/undotted index notation handles complex conjugation of spinors elegantly. What's the rule?

options: Undotted $\alpha = 1, 2$ for left $\psi^\alpha$; dotted $\dot\alpha$ for right $\bar\psi^{\dot\alpha}$; complex conjugation swaps dot/undot
Dotted and undotted indices are interchangeable in all contexts; they convey the same information about the spinor
The dot above an index indicates differentiation with respect to time, as in Newton's $\dot x$ notation for velocity
Only undotted indices represent physical spinor components; the dotted versions are mathematical artefacts of notation
passive.explanation: Standard in SUSY notation (Wess-Bagger). Complex conjugate $(\psi^\alpha)^* = \bar\psi^{\dot\alpha}$ swaps index type. Indices raised/lowered with antisymmetric $\epsilon_{\alpha\beta}, \epsilon^{\alpha\beta}$ (numerical $\pm 1$, two values). Contractions are $\psi\chi = \psi^\alpha\chi_\alpha = \epsilon_{\alpha\beta}\psi^\alpha\chi^\beta$ (antisymmetric — Grassmann variables anticommute, so $\psi\chi = -\chi\psi$). The Pauli matrices in Lorentz form: $(\sigma^\mu)_{\alpha\dot\beta} = (I, \vec\sigma)$, $(\bar\sigma^\mu)^{\dot\alpha\beta} = (I, -\vec\sigma)$.

question: Charge conjugation $\psi \to \psi^c$ converts a Dirac spinor to its antiparticle spinor. In Dirac representation, what is the operation?

options: $\psi^c = C\bar\psi^T$ with $C = i\gamma^2\gamma^0$; converts +energy electron solution to +energy positron solution
$\psi^c = -\psi$ — charge conjugation is just an overall sign flip with no gamma matrix structure required
$\psi^c = \psi^*$ — pure complex conjugation alone, with no involvement of gamma matrices or transposition
Charge conjugation does not exist for Dirac spinors; it is defined only for scalar fields and complex scalars
passive.explanation: $C$ satisfies $C\gamma^{\mu T}C^{-1} = -\gamma^\mu$, ensuring $\psi^c$ obeys the same Dirac equation with opposite charge sign. In QFT, $C$ is the antiunitary operator implementing charge conjugation on field operators: $\hat C a \hat C^{-1} = b$, exchanging particle and antiparticle. Majorana condition: $\psi^c = \psi$ — particle = antiparticle. For charged particles (electron), $\psi^c \neq \psi$ since they carry distinct charges.

question: Helicity is the projection of spin on the momentum direction: $h = \hat{\mathbf S}\cdot\hat{\mathbf p}/|\mathbf p|$. How is it related to chirality for massive vs massless particles?

options: Massless: helicity = chirality (Lorentz invariant). Massive: helicity is frame-dependent; chirality intrinsic but mixes via mass
Helicity and chirality are always identical for any particle regardless of whether it has mass or is massless
Helicity is intrinsic for any particle of any mass; chirality is the frame-dependent quantity instead
Neither helicity nor chirality concept applies meaningfully to spin-1/2 particles in relativistic dynamics
passive.explanation: For massless fermions: travel at $c$, no rest frame, helicity ±1/2 is invariant. For massive fermions: in rest frame, helicity is not defined ($\mathbf p = 0$); in moving frame, helicity ±1/2 depends on observer (a fast-moving observer sees opposite helicity). Photons (massless): helicity ±1 — only two physical polarisations (left/right circular). The chiral/helicity distinction is crucial in the weak interaction where only L-chiral states couple to W bosons.

question: Grassmann variables $\theta^\alpha$ are anticommuting spinor parameters in supersymmetry. What are the key algebraic rules?

options: $\{\theta^\alpha,\theta^\beta\} = 0$ (anticommute); Berezin $\int d\theta\,1 = 0$, $\int d\theta\,\theta = 1$; basis of SUSY superspace
Grassmann variables commute with each other like ordinary real or complex numbers in standard analysis
$\theta^\alpha\theta^\alpha$ is nonzero in general; products of identical Grassmann variables give nontrivial Grassmann squares
Berezin integral satisfies $\int d\theta\,1 = 1$ (normalised like the Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb R$ or the unit interval)
passive.explanation: Berezin's calculus (1966): integration over Grassmann variables defined so that $\int d\theta = 0$, $\int d\theta \theta = 1$ — opposite of ordinary integration. Gauss integral over Grassmann: $\int d\bar\eta d\eta e^{-\bar\eta M\eta} = \det M$ (vs $(\det M)^{-1/2}$ for bosonic). This is why fermionic path integrals give determinants in the denominator/numerator opposite to bosons. SUSY parameter $\theta^\alpha$ is anticommuting; superspace coordinates $(x^\mu, \theta^\alpha, \bar\theta^{\dot\alpha})$.

question: In string theory and supergravity, spinors in higher dimensions follow Bott periodicity (mod 8). What's the structure?

options: Spinor properties (Majorana/Weyl/Majorana-Weyl possible?) repeat mod 8; 2D and 10D both admit Majorana-Weyl spinors
Spinor structure is identical in all spacetime dimensions; only the matrix dimension grows with $d$ but properties unchanged
No higher-dimensional spinors exist beyond 4D Minkowski; the Dirac equation is unique to four spacetime dimensions
Bott periodicity applies only to even spacetime dimensions; odd dimensions have a different, non-periodic structure
passive.explanation: Bott periodicity in Clifford algebras: $\text{Cl}(d+8) \cong \text{Cl}(d) \otimes \mathbb R(16)$ (16-dim real matrix algebra). Implications: spinor dim is $2^{\lfloor d/2 \rfloor}$; reality (Majorana possible?) depends on $d \mod 8$. In particular: Majorana-Weyl spinors exist only for $d \equiv 2 \mod 8$ (so $d = 2, 10, 18$). Type IIA/B superstring: 10D Majorana-Weyl, 8 real degrees of freedom. M-theory: 11D Majorana, 32 real components.

question: Twistor theory (Penrose) replaces 4D spacetime with twistor space using spinor products. What are twistors?

options: Twistors $Z^A = (\omega^\alpha, \pi_{\dot\alpha})$ encode points and lightrays in Minkowski; modern use in BCFW amplitudes
Twistors are simply standard 4-vectors given a new name; no new geometric or analytical content beyond Minkowski space
Twistor space is 6-dimensional and entirely unrelated to spacetime geometry or scattering amplitudes
Twistors are useful only in 2D conformal field theory; they have no application to 4D Lorentz-invariant physics
passive.explanation: Penrose's twistor program (1967): twistors $Z^A$ are points in $\mathbb{CP}^3$; spacetime points are sets of twistors satisfying an incidence relation. Massless fields satisfy linear equations in twistor space → many simplifications. Modern resurgence: scattering amplitudes in $\mathcal N = 4$ super-Yang-Mills are best computed in twistor space (Witten, Cachazo, Hodges...). BCFW shift uses twistor variables to recursively build tree amplitudes. Connection to Grassmannian geometry: amplituhedron (Arkani-Hamed et al.).
